abstract: Photonic computing offers a transformative pathway toward high-speed, energy-efficient vision processing. Despite this potential, practical deployment has been hindered by the complexities of optical nonlinearities, the heavy power demands of the analog-to-digital converters (ADCs) required for digital post-processing, and a high sensitivity to systemic noise. In this work, we introduce ACCEL, an all-analog chip that fuses electronic and light-based computing to overcome these barriers. ACCEL achieves a systemic energy efficiency of 74.8 peta-operations per second per watt (PetaOPS/W) and a processing speed of 4.6 PetaOPS—with over 99% of tasks executed optically. These metrics represent improvements of three and one orders of magnitude, respectively, over current state-of-the-art processors. By utilizing diffractive optics as an inherent feature encoder, the resulting photocurrents are processed directly within an integrated analog circuit. This architecture bypasses the need for ADCs entirely, reducing frame latency to just 72 ns. Through the joint optimization of optoelectronic hardware and adaptive training protocols, ACCEL demonstrates experimental classification accuracies of 85.5% on Fashion-MNIST, 82.0% on 3-class ImageNet, and 92.6% for time-lapse video recognition. Furthermore, the system exhibits exceptional robustness under ultra-low-light conditions (0.14 fJ μm⁻² per frame). These capabilities position ACCEL as a versatile solution for power-constrained, high-throughput applications, including wearable electronics, autonomous vehicles, and industrial inspection systems.
